questions.text: Which one of the following is inorganic polymer:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Typical inorganic polymers are diamond, graphite, silicates, and other solids in which all atoms are connected by covalent bonds. A macromolecule is a very large molecule having a polymeric chain structure.

Some of the best-known examples of inorganic polymers include silicone rubber (polydimethylsiloxane), polyphosphazene, polysiloxanes, and polysilanes. Inorganic polymers offer valuable properties, such as high thermal stability, electrical conductivity, nonflammability, and low-temperature flexibility.

Deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) and ribonucleic acid (RNA) are polymers composed of monomers called nucleotides. An RNA nucleotide consists of a five-carbon sugar phosphate linked to one of four nucleic acid bases: guanine (G), cytosine (C), adenine (A) and uracil (U).

Proteins are natural polymer of α−amino acids. A definite sequence of amino acids form a specific protein. Two or more than two amino acids bind to give peptide bond. Proteins are polypeptide, which loses its biological activity by physical or chemical changes.

questions.explanation: Typical inorganic polymers are diamond, graphite, silicates, and other solids in which all atoms are connected by covalent bonds. A macromolecule is a very large molecule having a polymeric chain structure.

questions.text: Hardening of oil involves which of the following process:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Oxidation is the loss of electrons during a reaction by a molecule, atom or ion. Oxidation occurs when the oxidation state of a molecule, atom or ion is increased. The opposite process is called reduction, which occurs when there is a gain of electrons or the oxidation state of an atom, molecule, or ion decreases.
Hardening of oil is the other name of hydrogenation. The process of the formation of the saturated compound from the unsaturated compounds by adding hydrogen is called hydrogenation.

Saponification can be defined as a “hydration reaction where free hydroxide breaks the ester bonds between the fatty acids and glycerol of a triglyceride, resulting in free fatty acids and glycerol,” which are each soluble in aqueous solutions.

Hydrolysis reactions are the reverse of condensation reactions. In a hydrolysis reaction, a larger molecule forms two (or more) smaller molecules and water is consumed as a reactant. Hydrolysis ("hydro" = water and "lysis" = break) involves adding water to one large molecule to break it into multiple smaller molecules.

questions.explanation: Hardening of oil is the other name of hydrogenation. The process of the formation of the saturated compound from the unsaturated compounds by adding hydrogen is called hydrogenation.

questions.text: Which characteristic functional group is present in fats:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Traces of many aldehydes are found in essential oils and often contribute to their favorable odours, e.g. cinnamaldehyde, cilantro, and vanillin. Possibly because of the high reactivity of the formyl group, aldehydes are not common in several of the natural building blocks: amino acids, nucleic acids, lipids.

Traces of many aldehydes and ketones are found in essential oils and often contribute to their favorable odours, e.g. cinnamaldehyde, cilantro, and vanillin. Possibly because of the high reactivity of the formyl group, aldehydes are not common in several of the natural building blocks: amino acids, nucleic acids, lipids.
Carboxyl groups are commonly found in amino acids, fatty acids, and other biomolecules.
Ester groups are found in triglycerides (fats) such as tristearin.

questions.explanation: Ester groups are found in triglycerides (fats) such as tristearin.

questions.text: In which of the following case Zn2+ ions are used as co-factor:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Copper is an important micronutrient used by cytochrome c oxidase (Cox) as a metal cofactor. Cox's key complex includes a copper atom which is needed for its catalytic action. Complications in biogenesis and copper entry into cytochrome c correspond to mitochondrial human diseases.
Zinc is a cofactor for the enzyme carbonic anhydrase which is essential to carbon uptake during photosynthesis.
Glucose doesn't act as a cofactor.
During anaerobic respiration the conversion of pyruvate into acetaldehyde, along with co-enzyme TPP, the cofactor required is. Mg++.

questions.explanation: Zinc is a cofactor for the enzyme carbonic anhydrase which is essential to carbon uptake during photosynthesis.

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of associated lipid:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Stearin is a triglyceride saturated fat that is commonly found in animal products. The many carbon-hydrogen bonds in a lipid are referred to as a "hydrocarbon chain." Hydrocarbon chains are nonpolar, so do not interact well with polar substances like water.

Lipoproteins are round particles made of fat (lipids) and proteins that travel in your bloodstream to cells throughout your body. Cholesterol and triglycerides are two types of lipids found in lipoproteins. Your body makes different types of lipoproteins.

The functions of vitamin D are linked to lipid values. First, vitamin D regulates calcium metabolism and increases intestinal calcium absorption, thereby reducing intestinal fatty acid absorption [25]. Therefore, a reduction in intestinal fat absorption can lower the cholesterol level.

Lipids are an essential component of the cell membrane. The structure is typically made of a glycerol backbone, 2 fatty acid tails (hydrophobic), and a phosphate group (hydrophilic). As such, phospholipids are amphipathic.

questions.explanation: The functions of vitamin D are linked to lipid values. First, vitamin D regulates calcium metabolism and increases intestinal calcium absorption, thereby reducing intestinal fatty acid absorption [25]. Therefore, a reduction in intestinal fat absorption can lower the cholesterol level.

questions.text: Formation of Terylene and Nylon both are examples of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In organic chemistry, an addition reaction is an organic reaction in which two or more molecules combine to generate a bigger one (the adduct). Molecules with carbon—hetero double bonds, such as carbonyl (C=O) or imine (C=N) groups, can be added because they have double-bond character as well.
Elimination reactions involve the removal of two atoms or groups of atoms from a molecule to form a new product. One example of an elimination reaction is the dehydration of alcohols to form alkenes.

Terylene is a condensation co polymer formed by the monomers like ethylene glycol and terephthalic acid. These two monomers undergo condensation reaction and by eliminating a water molecule the polymer chain, terylene is formed. During this polymerization process an ester bond is formed between the monomers.

Terylene is a condensation co polymer formed by the monomers like ethylene glycol and terephthalic acid. These two monomers undergo condensation reaction and by eliminating a water molecule the polymer chain, terylene is formed. During this polymerization process an ester bond is formed between the monomers.

questions.explanation: Terylene is a condensation co polymer formed by the monomers like ethylene glycol and terephthalic acid. These two monomers undergo condensation reaction and by eliminating a water molecule the polymer chain, terylene is formed. During this polymerization process an ester bond is formed between the monomers.

questions.text: Quantitatively unsaturation of oils is determined by:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Iodine value can be used to measure the degree of unsaturation of oils and fats. The results are normally expressed as the number of grams of iodine absorbed by 100 g of oil or fat, considering the conditions of the test.

Iodine value can be used to measure the degree of unsaturation of oils and fats. The results are normally expressed as the number of grams of iodine absorbed by 100 g of oil or fat, considering the conditions of the test.

Iodine value can be used to measure the degree of unsaturation of oils and fats. The results are normally expressed as the number of grams of iodine absorbed by 100 g of oil or fat, considering the conditions of the test.

Iodine value can be used to measure the degree of unsaturation of oils and fats. The results are normally expressed as the number of grams of iodine absorbed by 100 g of oil or fat, considering the conditions of the test.

questions.text: Cholesterol belong to:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Vitamins are organic molecules that are essential to an organism in small quantities for proper metabolic function. Essential nutrients cannot be synthesized in the organism in sufficient quantities for survival, and therefore must be obtained through the diet.

Carbohydrates, or carbs, are sugar molecules. Along with proteins and fats, carbohydrates are one of three main nutrients found in foods and drinks. Your body breaks down carbohydrates into glucose. Glucose, or blood sugar, is the main source of energy for your body's cells, tissues, and organs.

Cholesterol, triglycerides, and high-density lipoproteins are important constituents of the lipid fraction of the human body. Cholesterol is an unsaturated alcohol of the steroid family of compounds; it is essential for the normal function of all animal cells and is a fundamental element of their cell membranes.

:Cholesterol, triglycerides, and high-density lipoproteins are important constituents of the lipid fraction of the human body. Cholesterol is an unsaturated alcohol of the steroid family of compounds; it is essential for the normal function of all animal cells and is a fundamental element of their cell membranes.

questions.explanation: :Cholesterol, triglycerides, and high-density lipoproteins are important constituents of the lipid fraction of the human body. Cholesterol is an unsaturated alcohol of the steroid family of compounds; it is essential for the normal function of all animal cells and is a fundamental element of their cell membranes.

questions.text: Amino acids in protein are linked together by:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The bond that forms between the oxygen and carbon atoms is then known as an ester bond or ester linkage. Many everyday products such as cosmetics, flavoring agents for beverages, and industrial cleaning agents contain esters. While these are usually formed in a lab, esters also occur naturally.

Each amino acid is linked to the next amino acid through peptide bonds created during the protein biosynthesis process. The two ends of each polypeptide chain are known as the amino terminus (N-terminus) and the carboxyl terminus (C-terminus).

Ethers feature bent C−O−C linkages. In dimethyl ether, the bond angle is 111° and C–O distances are 141 pm. The barrier to rotation about the C–O bonds is low. The bonding of oxygen in ethers, alcohols, and water is similar.

A glycosidic bond or glycosidic linkage is a type of ether bond that joins a carbohydrate molecule to another group, which may or may not be another carbohydrate. A glycosidic bond is formed between the hemiacetal or hemiketal group of a saccharide and the hydroxyl group of some compound such as an alcohol.

questions.explanation: Each amino acid is linked to the next amino acid through peptide bonds created during the protein biosynthesis process. The two ends of each polypeptide chain are known as the amino terminus (N-terminus) and the carboxyl terminus (C-terminus).

questions.text: The colour which cellulose can give with I2 solution:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Starch gives blue colour and glycogen gives red colour with iodine solution.

When iodine solution is added to leaves, colour changes from green to blue indicating the presence of starch. But presence of chlorophyll can disguise the result. To avoid this, chlorophyll is removed before the test.
Starch gives blue colour and glycogen gives red colour with iodine solution.
Because cellulose does not have a helical structure, it does not bind to iodine to form a colored product.

questions.explanation: Because cellulose does not have a helical structure, it does not bind to iodine to form a colored product.

questions.text: Which one of the following plastic is a thermo setting plastic:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) is one type of thermoplastic polymer that is currently penetrating many aspects of life through its wide use, and has become a universal polymer. From: Use of Recycled Plastics in Eco-efficient Concrete, 2019.
Polystyrene (PS) is a polymer made from the monomer styrene, a liquid hydrocarbon that is commercially manufactured from petroleum.

Polyethylene, also known as polythene or polyethene, is one of the most commonly used plastics in the world. Polyethylenes usually have a linear structure and are known to be addition polymers. The primary application of these synthetic polymers is in packaging.
Bakelite is a cross-linked polymer which after heating cannot be recovered into its original structure. Hence, it is a thermosetting polymer.

questions.explanation: Bakelite is a cross-linked polymer which after heating cannot be recovered into its original structure. Hence, it is a thermosetting polymer.

questions.text: Which one of the following polymer is called a polyamide:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Monomer of rayon is cellulose, which reacts carbon disulfide (CS2) and base (sodium hydroxide) to give a soluble polymer that can be converted into rayon.
Orlon is the name that is used in trade purposes for Polyacrylonitrile (PAN). It is a polymer of acrylonitrile which is commonly known as vinyl cyanide (VCN).

Nylon is a polyamide polymer characterized by the presence of amide groups in the main polymer chain.Nylon 6 is produced from caprolactam by ring-opening polymerization.Since caprolactam contains six carbon atoms and only one monomer is used, the polymer is thus designated as nylon 6.
Terylene is a synthetic polymer that is prepared through the polymerization of ethylene glycol and terephthalic acid

questions.explanation: Nylon is a polyamide polymer characterized by the presence of amide groups in the main polymer chain.Nylon 6 is produced from caprolactam by ring-opening polymerization.Since caprolactam contains six carbon atoms and only one monomer is used, the polymer is thus designated as nylon 6.

questions.text: Which of the following enzymes bring about the hydrolysis of fats:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Urease is a metalloenzyme evenly distributed in the human body, including in the CNS, responsible for catalyzing the hydrolysis of urea into carbon dioxide and ammonia, the latter of which can be protonated to form ammonium, resulting in an increase in pH with consequences to pathogen-host interaction

maltase, enzyme that catalyzes the hydrolysis of the disaccharide maltose to the simple sugar glucose. The enzyme is found in plants, bacteria, and yeast; in humans and other vertebrates it is thought to be synthesized by cells of the mucous membrane lining the intestinal wall.

Zymase (also known as alcoholase) is an obsolete term for an enzyme complex that catalyzes the fermentation of sugar into ethanol and carbon dioxide. It occurs naturally in yeasts. Zymase activity varies among yeast strains. Zymase is also the brand name of the drug pancrelipase.

Lipase is an enzyme that catalysts the hydrolysis of fat. Fat digestion mainly takes place in the small intestine with the help of lipase enzymes. Lipase enzyme is produced in small amounts by the mouth and stomach, and in larger amounts by the pancreas.

questions.explanation: Lipase is an enzyme that catalysts the hydrolysis of fat. Fat digestion mainly takes place in the small intestine with the help of lipase enzymes. Lipase enzyme is produced in small amounts by the mouth and stomach, and in larger amounts by the pancreas.

questions.text: Which one of the following is a water soluble vitamin:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Vitamins can classify as water-soluble or fat-soluble. The fat-soluble vitamins include vitamins A, D, E, and K. Fat-soluble vitamins play integral roles in a multitude of physiological processes such as vision, bone health, immune function, and coagulation.
Vitamin D is a fat-soluble vitamin that depends on the gut's ability to absorb dietary fat. People who are obese tend to have lower blood vitamin D levels.

Water-soluble precursors of vitamin E (α-tocopherol, αT), such as its esters with acetate (αTA), succinate (αTS), or phosphate (αTP), have increased solubility in water and stability against reaction with free radicals, but they are rapidly converted during their uptake into the lipid-soluble vitamin E.

Water-soluble vitamins are carried to the body's tissues but are not stored in the body. They are found in many plant and animal foods and in dietary supplements and must be taken in daily. Vitamin C and members of the vitamin B complex are water-soluble.

questions.explanation: Water-soluble vitamins are carried to the body's tissues but are not stored in the body. They are found in many plant and animal foods and in dietary supplements and must be taken in daily. Vitamin C and members of the vitamin B complex are water-soluble.

questions.text: Which one of the following element is not present in all proteins:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Carbon is present in all organic compounds, including proteins, as it forms the backbone of amino acids, the building blocks of proteins.
Hydrogen is also present in all organic compounds, including proteins. It is bonded to carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur atoms within the protein structure.
Nitrogen is an essential element found in all proteins. It is part of the amine group (-NH2) present in each amino acid, which forms the peptide bonds linking amino acids together in proteins.

While sulfur is commonly found in many proteins, not all proteins contain sulfur. Some proteins, particularly those with a high content of hydrophobic amino acids, may lack sulfur-containing amino acids such as cysteine and methionine. Therefore, option D is correct as sulfur is not universally present in all proteins.

questions.explanation: While sulfur is commonly found in many proteins, not all proteins contain sulfur. Some proteins, particularly those with a high content of hydrophobic amino acids, may lack sulfur-containing amino acids such as cysteine and methionine. Therefore, option D is correct as sulfur is not universally present in all proteins.

questions.text: Which one of the following is purine:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: There are three pyrimidine bases found in nucleic acids: thymine (T), cytosine (C), and Uracil (U). These bases are substituted pyrimidines as they have molecular formulas which have been slightly altered from the generic C4 H4 N2 formula (Figure 2). Figure 2 The nitrogenous bases found in DNA and RNA.

LThere are three pyrimidine bases found in nucleic acids: thymine (T), cytosine (C), and Uracil (U). These bases are substituted pyrimidines as they have molecular formulas which have been slightly altered from the generic C4 H4 N2 formula (Figure 2). Figure 2 The nitrogenous bases found in DNA and RNA.

The most important biological substituted purines are adenine and guanine, which are the major purine bases found in RNA and DNA. In DNA, guanine and adenine base pair (see Watson-Crick pairing) with cytosine and thymine (see pyrimidines) respectively.

There are three pyrimidine bases found in nucleic acids: thymine (T), cytosine (C), and Uracil (U). These bases are substituted pyrimidines as they have molecular formulas which have been slightly altered from the generic C4 H4 N2 formula (Figure 2). Figure 2 The nitrogenous bases found in DNA and RNA.

questions.explanation: They are nitrogenous bases that make up the two different nucleotides in DNA and RNA. Purines (adenine and guanine) are two-carbon nitrogen ring bases while pyrimidines (cytosine and thymine) are one-carbon nitrogen ring bases.

questions.text: Which one of the following is not a fatty acid:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Oleic acid is a mono-unsaturated omega-9 fatty acid found in various animal and vegetable sources. Triglyceride esters of oleic acid comprise the majority of olive oil.
Stearic acid is a prevalent fatty-acid in nature, found in many animal and vegetable fats, but is usually higher in animal fat than vegetable fat. It has a melting point of 69.4 °C and a pKa of 4.50.
This compound belongs to the class of organic compounds known as benzoic acids. These are organic Compounds containing a benzene ring which bears at least one carboxyl group.
Butyric acid is a straight-chain saturated fatty acid that is butane in which one of the terminal methyl groups has been oxidised to a carboxy group.

questions.explanation: This compound belongs to the class of organic compounds known as benzoic acids. These are organic Compounds containing a benzene ring which bears at least one carboxyl group.

questions.text: Which of the following is optimum temperature of most of enzymes:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The optimum temperature for most enzymes is about 98.6 degrees Fahrenheit (37 degrees Celsius). There are also enzymes that work well at lower and higher temperatures. For example, Arctic animals have enzymes adapted to lower optimal temperatures; animals in desert climates have enzymes adapted to higher temperatures.

The optimum temperature for most enzymes is about 98.6 degrees Fahrenheit (37 degrees Celsius). There are also enzymes that work well at lower and higher temperatures. For example, Arctic animals have enzymes adapted to lower optimal temperatures; animals in desert climates have enzymes adapted to higher temperatures.

questions.text: Which of the following is the most abundant organic substance found in nature:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Fructose also occurs naturally in abundance in fruits (Table 1) and in lesser amounts in tuberous vegetables such as onions and potatoes. These sources alone contribute some 40–60% of an individual's total fructose intake

Starch is the most important source of carbohydrates in the human diet and accounts for more than 50% of our carbohydrate intake. It occurs in plants in the form of granules, and these are particularly abundant in seeds (especially the cereal grains) and tubers, where they serve as a storage form of carbohydrates.
Cellulose is the most abundant organic compound on Earth.

Glucose is the most abundant carbohydrate in peripheral circulation, and blood glucose is the most common analysis done in the clinical laboratory. The body actively maintains blood glucose levels between 80 to 120 mg/dl through metabolic processes such as gluconeogenesis, glycogenolysis, glycolysis, and glycogenesis.

questions.explanation: Cellulose is the most abundant organic compound on Earth.

questions.text: Cholesterol is an important precursor in the biosynthesis of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Cholesterol is an essential component of cell membranes and the precursor for the synthesis of steroid hormones and bile acids.
Cholesterol is an essential component of cell membranes and the precursor for the synthesis of steroid hormones and bile acids.
Cholesterol is an essential component of cell membranes and the precursor for the synthesis of steroid hormones and bile acids.
Cholesterol is an essential component of cell membranes and the precursor for the synthesis of steroid hormones and bile acids.

questions.text: Vegetable oils are:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Unsaturated fats are predominantly found in foods from plants, such as vegetable oils, nuts, and seeds.
Unsaturated fats are predominantly found in foods from plants, such as vegetable oils, nuts, and seeds.
Unsaturated fats are predominantly found in foods from plants, such as vegetable oils, nuts, and seeds.
Unsaturated fats are predozminantly found in foods from plants, such as vegetable oils, nuts, and seeds.

questions.text: The fiber which is made from acrylonitrile as monomer:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Acrylic fibre is synthesised through polymerisation of acrylonitrile (vinyl cyanide) and has the chemical name polyacrylonitrile (PAN) with the empirical formula where 'n' is the degree of polymerisation.

Polyester fibre is a “manufactured fibre in which the fibre forming substance is any long chain synthetic polymer composed at least 85% by weight of an ester of a dihydric alcohol (HOROH) and terephthalic acid (p–HOOC–C6H4COOH)”.

Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) is one type of thermoplastic polymer that is currently penetrating many aspects of life through its wide use, and has become a universal polymer. From: Use of Recycled Plastics in Eco-efficient.

Rayon is described as a regenerated fibre because the cellulose, obtained from soft woods or from the short fibres (linters) that adhere to cottonseeds, is converted to a liquid compound, squeezed through tiny holes in a device called a spinnerette, and then converted back to cellulose in the form of fibre.

questions.explanation: Acrylic fibre is synthesised through polymerisation of acrylonitrile (vinyl cyanide) and has the chemical name polyacrylonitrile (PAN) with the empirical formula where 'n' is the degree of polymerisation.

questions.text: Which of these enzymes is an acidic pH active enzymes:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Gastric lipase is an acidic lipase secreted by the gastric chief cells in the fundic mucosa in the stomach. It has a pH optimum of 3–6.
In the exocrine pancreas, amylase is one of the major zymogens. It is a basic protein of pI 8.6 and does not precipitate in acidic conditions.

Pepsin is an aspartic protease that acts in food digestion in the mammal stomach. An optimal pH of around 2 allows pepsin to operate in its natural acidic environment, while at neutral pH the protein is denatured.

The purified trypsin belonged to an alkaline protease, as it was highly active between pH 8.0 and 10.0 with the optimum pH of 9.0. Meanwhile, the enzyme exhibited outstanding stability at pHs 8.0, 9.0, and 10.0, while it could even retain about 50% residual activity after 80 h under the pH condition of 10.0

questions.explanation: Pepsin is an aspartic protease that acts in food digestion in the mammal stomach. An optimal pH of around 2 allows pepsin to operate in its natural acidic environment, while at neutral pH the protein is denatured.

questions.text: Which of these polymers is an addition polymer:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: An addition polymer is a polymer that forms by simple linking of monomers without the co-generation of other products. Addition polymerization differs from condensation polymerization, which does co-generate a product, usually water. Neoprene, Teflon, Polyvinylchloride are examples of addition polymers.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a polymer:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Starch is made of glucose monomers and occurs in both straight-chain and branched forms. Amylose is the straight-chain form, and consists of hundreds of linked glucose molecules. The branched form of starch is called amylopectin.

Glucose (also known as dextrose) is a basic monomer of carbohydrates 13 . Carbohydrates are classified based on the number of carbons they have. Glucose, galactose and fructose all have a six-carbon ring and the same formula; they are hexoses and isomers of one another.
Proteins are polymers of amino acids linked via α-peptide bonds

Nylon is a generic designation for a family of synthetic polymers composed of polyamide, which are repeating units linked by amide links. Nylon is a silk-like thermoplastic, usually made from petroleum, that can be melt-processed into fibers, films, or shapes.

questions.text: Starch is a polymer of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Starch is primarily composed of α–D–glucose units linked together. While β–D–glucose is a glucose isomer, it is not the primary monomer in starch.

Starch is a polysaccharide composed of repeating units of alpha-glucose molecules joined together through glycosidic bonds. It is the principal energy storage carbohydrate in plants, and is commonly found in grains, potatoes, and other starchy vegetables. The alpha-glucose molecules in starch can be arranged in two forms: amylose, which is a linear chain of alpha-glucose molecules linked by alpha-1, 4-glycosidic bonds, and amylopectin, which is a branched chain of alpha-glucose molecules linked by alpha-1, 4-glycosidic bonds and alpha-1, 6-glycosidic bonds.
γ–D–glucose is not a common form of glucose found in natural carbohydrates.
Starch is composed of α–D–glucose units, not α–L–glucose. The L-form of glucose is not typically found in biological carbohydrates.

questions.explanation: Starch is a polysaccharide composed of repeating units of alpha-glucose molecules joined together through glycosidic bonds. questions.text: The substance that retard the activity of enzyme is called:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The catalytic activity of certain enzymes is reversibly altered by certain inorganic and organic molecules called Modifiers. Those molecules which increase the enzyme activity are called as positive modifiers or enzyme activators and those which decrease the enzyme activity are called enzyme inhibitors.

Those molecules which increase the enzyme activity are called as positive modifiers or enzyme activators and those which decrease the enzyme activity are called enzyme inhibitors.

Those molecules which increase the enzyme activity are called as positive modifiers or enzyme activators and those which decrease the enzyme activity are called enzyme inhibitors.

questions.text: Which one of the following is not a polymer:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Glycogen is a glucose polymer that is primarily stored in the liver and muscle and is required for glucose homeostasis during fasting.

Starch is an enormous polymer of glucose subunits and is the capacity type of glucose in plants. Sources incorporate seeds, grains, corn, beans, potatoes, and rice. There are really two kinds of starch: amylose and amylopectin. Amylose is a long, unbranched, chain of glucose subunits.
Teflon is a polymer of tetrafluoroethene. It is an excellent plastic material for films and coatings.

Synthetic polymers are derived from petroleum oil, and made by scientists and engineers. Examples of synthetic polymers include nylon, polyethylene, polyester, Teflon, and epoxy. Natural polymers occur in nature and can be extracted. They are often water-based.

questions.explanation: Synthetic polymers are derived from petroleum oil, and made by scientists and engineers. Examples of synthetic polymers include nylon, polyethylene, polyester, Teflon, and epoxy. Natural polymers occur in nature and can be extracted. They are often water-based.

questions.text: The molecular formula of a trisaccharide will be:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The chemical formula of a trisaccharide made of three bonded glucose molecules is C18H32O16. A trisaccharide composed of three glucose molecules would be formed through glycosidic linkages, which connect individual sugar units. Glucose, a monosaccharide, has the chemical formula C6H12O6.

questions.text: All fats are:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Fats and oils are composed of molecules known as triglycerides, which are esters composed of three fatty acid units linked to glycerol. An increase in the percentage of shorter-chain fatty acids and/or unsaturated fatty acids lowers the melting point of a fat or oil.

questions.text: Which one of the following is a co-polymer:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) is one type of thermoplastic polymer that is currently penetrating many aspects of life through its wide use, and has become a universal polymer. From: Use of Recycled Plastics in Eco-efficient.

Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is a water-soluble plastic commercially used in laundry and dish detergent pods (LDPs) for which a complete understanding of its fate in the environment and subsequent consequences is lacking.

Polyethylene or polythene (abbreviated PE; IUPAC name polyethene or poly(methylene)) is the most commonly produced plastic. It is a polymer, primarily used for packaging (plastic bags, plastic films, geomembranes and containers including bottles, etc.).
Nylon-12/6/66 is a copolymer formed from the monomers used in the manufacture of Nylon-12, Nylon-6, and Nylon-66.

questions.explanation: Nylon-12/6/66 is a copolymer formed from the monomers used in the manufacture of Nylon-12, Nylon-6, and Nylon-66.

questions.text: Glycosidic linkage is present in:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Proteins are polymers of amino acids linked via α-peptide bonds.

Nylon 6,6 is a synthetic polymer which is a polyamide or nylon. The nylon 6,6 is formed of two monomer units each containing 6 carbon atoms. The two monomers are hexamethylenediamine and adipic acid which combine to give nylon 6,6
Carbohydrates. Disaccharides and polysaccharides are formed when monosaccharides bond along with glycosidic linkages in carbohydrates.
DNA is a polymer of nucleotides which are linked to each other by 3-5 phosphodiester bond.

questions.explanation: Carbohydrates. Disaccharides and polysaccharides are formed when monosaccharides bond along with glycosidic linkages in carbohydrates.

questions.text: In which of these processes are small organic molecules made into macromolecules:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: cracking, in petroleum refining, the process by which heavy hydrocarbon molecules are broken up into lighter molecules by means of heat and usually pressure and sometimes catalysts. Cracking is the most important process for the commercial production of gasoline and diesel fuel. cracking.

options.text: The fractional distillation of crude oil
options.explanation: Fractional distillation of crude oil is the process by which we obtain all the various petroleum products that we rely on from crude oil – whether that's internal combustion engine fuels such as the petrol or diesel that fuels our cars' engines; the gas oil powering machinery and furnaces; the jet fuel that fuels .
During the polymerisation of ethene, thousands of ethene molecules join together to make poly(ethene) - commonly called polythene. Ethene is known as the monomer. Poly(ethene) is the polymer.

Protein hydrolysis is the process by which peptide bonds are broken under acidic conditions at high temperatures, generating free amino acid residues. The resulting hydrolysates can then be analyzed by amino acid analysis to determine the amino acid composition and concentration of proteins.

questions.explanation: During the polymerisation of ethene, thousands of ethene molecules join together to make poly(ethene) - commonly called polythene. Ethene is known as the monomer. Poly(ethene) is the polymer.

questions.text: A polymeric substance that is formed in the liquid state and then hardened to a rigid solids is called a:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Fiber is a type of carbohydrate that the body can't digest. Though most carbohydrates are broken down into sugar molecules called glucose, fiber cannot be broken down into sugar molecules, and instead it passes through the body undigested.

a synthetic material made from a wide range of organic polymers such as polyethylene, PVC, nylon, etc., that can be moulded into shape while soft, and then set into a rigid or slightly elastic form.Resins are vicious, liquid polymers derived from organic or synthetic sources. Their benefit centres on their ability to transition from a liquid into a customisable solid, homogeneous structure.

varnish, liquid coating material containing a resin that dries to a hard transparent film. Most varnishes are a blend of resin, drying oil, drier, and volatile solvent. When varnish dries, its solvent portion evaporates, and the remaining constituents oxidize or polymerize to form a durable transparent film.

Polyamide (PA) resins are a family of thermoplastic polymers (Nylon, Kevlar®), which means that they may be formed with heat and re-melted without losing their intrinsic characteristics. Thermoplastics differ in this way from thermosets, which undergo permanent change after hardening.

questions.explanation: A synthetic material made from a wide range of organic polymers such as polyethylene, PVC, nylon, etc., that can be moulded into shape while soft, and then set into a rigid or slightly elastic form.Resins are vicious, liquid polymers derived from organic or synthetic sources. Their benefit centres on their ability to transition from a liquid into a customisable solid, homogeneous structure.

questions.text: Which one of the following elements is not present in all proteins:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Proteins are made of amino acids.Proteins are made up of amino acids containing mostly hydrogen, carbon, nitrogen and oxygen, as linked together in chains. questions.text: Which one of the following enzymes brings about the hydrolysis of fats:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Urease is a metalloenzyme evenly distributed in the human body, including in the CNS, responsible for catalyzing the hydrolysis of urea into carbon dioxide and ammonia, the latter of which can be protonated to form ammonium, resulting in an increase in pH with consequences to pathogen-host interaction

maltase, enzyme that catalyzes the hydrolysis of the disaccharide maltose to the simple sugar glucose. The enzyme is found in plants, bacteria, and yeast; in humans and other vertebrates it is thought to be synthesized by cells of the mucous membrane lining the intestinal wall.

Zymase (also known as alcoholase) is an obsolete term for an enzyme complex that catalyzes the fermentation of sugar into ethanol and carbon dioxide. It occurs naturally in yeasts. Zymase activity varies among yeast strains. Zymase is also the brand name of the drug pancrelipase.

Lipase is an enzyme that catalysts the hydrolysis of fat. Fat digestion mainly takes place in the small intestine with the help of lipase enzymes. Lipase enzyme is produced in small amounts by the mouth and stomach, and in larger amounts by the pancreas.

questions.explanation: Lipase is an enzyme that catalysts the hydrolysis of fat. Fat digestion mainly takes place in the small intestine with the help of lipase enzymes. Lipase enzyme is produced in small amounts by the mouth and stomach, and in larger amounts by the pancreas.

questions.text: The reaction between fat and NaOH is called:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Esterification is the process of combining an organic acid (RCOOH) with an alcohol (ROH) to form an ester (RCOOR) and water; or a chemical reaction resulting in the formation of at least one ester product. Ester is obtained by an esterification reaction of an alcohol and a carboxylic acid.

Hydrogenolysis is a chemical reaction whereby a carbon–carbon or carbon–heteroatom single bond is cleaved or undergoes lysis (breakdown) by hydrogen. The heteroatom may vary, but it usually is oxygen, nitrogen, or sulfur.

Fermentation is the breaking down of sugar molecules into simpler compounds to produce substances that can be used in making chemical energy. Chemical energy, typically in the form of ATP, is important as it drives various biological processes. Fermentation does not use oxygen; thus, it is “anaerobic”.

Saponification is a process involving the hydrolysis of fats on its reaction with alkali, thereby leading to the formation of salts of fatty acids and glycerol. The salts of fatty acids are known as soap. The amount of potassium hydroxide required in the hydrolysis indicates the saponification value of fat.

questions.explanation: Saponification is a process involving the hydrolysis of fats on its reaction with alkali, thereby leading to the formation of salts of fatty acids and glycerol. The salts of fatty acids are known as soap. The amount of potassium hydroxide required in the hydrolysis indicates the saponification value of fat.

options.explanation: Fructose and glucose are both simple monosaccharide sugars.
Fructose and glucose are both simple monosaccharide sugars.
Fructose and glucose are both simple monosaccharide sugars.
Fructose and glucose are both simple monosaccharide sugars.
